research_question: How can we reliably detect and localize defects in ultra-high-resolution (6559×6559) grayscale semiconductor images using only defect-free normal reference patches, while maintaining robustness to style, contrast, and noise variations under strict computational and time constraints?
background: Semiconductor manufacturing demands pixel-level defect inspection on wafer and die images, yet actual defect samples are extremely scarce, diverse, and prohibitively expensive to annotate, rendering supervised approaches infeasible at production scale. Existing anomaly detection methods such as memory-bank-based patch matching and pretrained feature comparison have shown promise on standard benchmarks (e.g., MVTec AD), but they have not been rigorously adapted to ultra-high-resolution single-channel industrial images that exhibit significant inter-lot style drift, equipment-induced brightness variation, and process noise. A training-light, reference-based anomaly detection system that operates within real-time manufacturing constraints would significantly reduce inspection costs and enable rapid adaptation across fabrication lines without retraining.

image_analysis.input_characteristics: Ultra-high-resolution 6559×6559 single-channel (grayscale) semiconductor images with fine-grained circuit textures, repetitive but locally varying patterns, process-induced noise, and equipment-dependent brightness/contrast shifts. Reference patches are defect-free crops of 256×256 or 512×512 pixels.
image_analysis.target_characteristics: Full-resolution anomaly score map or heatmap over the 6559×6559 source image, with defect candidates presented as bounding boxes or binary masks, accompanied by per-region defect confidence scores.
image_analysis.visual_gap: Input is a raw high-resolution grayscale image with no defect annotations; output is a spatially aligned anomaly map highlighting subtle deviations (sub-pixel to small-region defects such as scratches, particles, pattern breaks) against a highly textured normal background, requiring disambiguation of true defects from normal process variation and noise.

problem_analysis.core_problem: Detecting subtle, diverse, and rare defects in ultra-high-resolution grayscale semiconductor images without defect labels, using only normal reference patches, while handling domain shift from style/noise/equipment variations and meeting strict latency requirements.
current_limitations: Standard anomaly detection models (PatchCore, PaDiM, SPADE) are designed for moderate-resolution RGB images and do not natively handle 6559×6559 grayscale inputs or efficient patch-based tiling/merging at this scale

Pretrained feature extractors (ImageNet-based CNNs, ViTs) are optimized for 3-channel natural images, causing domain mismatch with single-channel semiconductor textures and potential loss of fine-grained defect features
Memory bank approaches suffer from high memory consumption and slow retrieval at the scale needed for ultra-high-resolution images with dense patch sampling
Rule-based and pixel-level comparison methods are highly sensitive to brightness, contrast, and noise variations, producing unacceptable false positive rates
Existing methods lack mechanisms for online style/domain adaptation without retraining when switching between lots, equipment, or process conditions
Boundary artifacts and inconsistent scoring at patch seams degrade full-image anomaly map quality after patch-based splitting and merging
problem_analysis.why_difficult: The problem combines multiple compounding challenges: (1) the extreme image resolution (43M pixels) prohibits whole-image processing and demands efficient tiling with seamless merging, (2) grayscale single-channel input reduces discriminative feature richness compared to RGB, (3) defects are extremely rare, small, and visually subtle against complex repetitive textures, (4) inter-image domain shift from equipment/process variation inflates false positives, (5) the absence of defect labels eliminates supervised calibration of detection thresholds, and (6) real-time constraints (~1 minute per image on a single GPU) severely limit computational budget for feature extraction and matching across thousands of patches.

in_scope: Patch-based splitting and merging strategies for 6559×6559 grayscale images with overlap and boundary artifact mitigation
Reference-based anomaly detection using defect-free normal patches as the sole training signal
Pretrained frozen feature extractor adaptation for grayscale semiconductor domain (channel replication, grayscale-aware fine-tuning, or domain-specific lightweight adapters)
Memory bank construction, indexing, and efficient nearest-neighbor retrieval for patch-level anomaly scoring
Style/contrast/noise normalization or invariant feature extraction to handle domain shift across lots and equipment
Anomaly map generation, score calibration, and defect localization (bounding boxes and masks)
Inference optimization for single-GPU deployment under 1-minute latency constraint
Few-shot or zero-shot adaptation to new production conditions by updating normal reference sets without retraining
out_scope: Supervised defect classification or segmentation with large labeled defect datasets
End-to-end fine-tuning of large foundation models (e.g., full fine-tuning of ViT-Large, SAM, or diffusion models)
Defect root cause analysis or process control feedback
Synthetic defect generation or data augmentation as primary detection method
Real-time video stream processing or multi-frame temporal analysis

quantitative: False Positive Rate on normal images ≤ 1% (ideally ≤ 0.5%)
False Alarm per normal image ≤ 0.1 on average
Anomalous Area Ratio on normal images ≤ 0.01%
Mean Absolute Score Shift under style/noise perturbation ≤ 0.05 (normalized score)
Threshold robustness: consistent detection performance across ±20% threshold variation
Inference time per 6559×6559 source image ≤ 60 seconds on single GPU
Peak GPU memory usage ≤ 24 GB (single consumer/workstation GPU)
Pixel-level AUROC ≥ 0.95 on defect-containing test images (when ground truth available)
qualitative: Anomaly maps should produce clean, interpretable heatmaps with sharp defect localization and minimal background noise
System should adapt to new lots, equipment, or process styles by simply adding normal reference patches without retraining
Detected defect regions should correspond to visually meaningful anomalies as validated by domain experts
Patch boundary artifacts in the merged full-image anomaly map should be imperceptible
The pipeline should be modular, allowing easy swap of feature extractors, similarity metrics, and normalization strategies

constraints: Input: single 6559×6559 grayscale (1-channel) semiconductor source image
Reference: defect-free normal patches of 256×256 or 512×512 pixels (or smaller)
Input and output spatial dimensions may differ (output anomaly map may be at reduced resolution)
Defect annotations are extremely limited or entirely absent — normal-only training paradigm required
Training should be minimal: normal-only, few-shot, or training-free approaches preferred
Single GPU (≤24 GB VRAM) or limited compute environment
End-to-end inspection time per source image must be under 60 seconds
Must be robust to inter-image style, contrast, brightness, and noise level variations
No end-to-end fine-tuning of very large foundation models (frozen backbones with lightweight adapters acceptable)
False positive rate must be practically low enough for real-world production deployment
System must support lot/equipment changeover with minimal or no retraining
target_metrics: False Positive Rate (FPR) on Normal Images
Anomalous Area Ratio on Normal Images
Score Stability under Style/Noise Perturbation (Mean Absolute Score Shift)
Threshold Robustness (performance consistency across threshold range)
expected_challenges: Adapting ImageNet-pretrained feature extractors to grayscale semiconductor textures without full fine-tuning: single-channel inputs and highly repetitive circuit patterns differ drastically from natural RGB images, potentially causing the frozen features to miss subtle defect-relevant texture variations while being over-sensitive to irrelevant process noise.

Managing the computational and memory cost of dense patch-level feature extraction and nearest-neighbor retrieval across ~650+ overlapping patches per source image within the 1-minute time budget on a single GPU, requiring careful balancing of patch overlap ratio, feature dimensionality, coreset size, and retrieval algorithm efficiency.

Achieving robust anomaly scoring under significant domain shift (brightness, contrast, noise, equipment differences) without retraining: style normalization or invariant feature design must suppress normal variation while preserving sensitivity to genuine defects, and threshold calibration must generalize across production conditions to keep false positives acceptably low.

input.topic: AI-based defect detection in high-resolution grayscale semiconductor images using normal-only reference patches
input.details: Develop an anomaly detection system that automatically locates defects in 6559x6559 grayscale semiconductor source images by leveraging only defect-free reference patches (256x256 or 512x512), employing approaches such as reference-based feature matching, memory bank patch anomaly localization, and pretrained frozen feature extractors, without requiring large-scale defect labeling.
input.problem_definition: Source images are ultra-high-resolution (6559x6559) grayscale semiconductor images that cannot be processed at once and require patch-based splitting and merging strategies. Actual defect samples are extremely scarce, diverse, and expensive to label, making supervised detection infeasible. Additionally, variations in image style, brightness, process noise, and equipment differences cause normal patterns to appear differently, leading to high false positives in rule-based methods and making pixel-level comparison alone insufficient for detecting very small defects and subtle texture differences.
input.desired_outcome: The system should reliably detect defect candidates using only normal reference patches and generate an anomaly map or defect score map over the full source image, presenting defect locations as bounding boxes or masks. It must maintain robust performance under style and noise variations, complete inspection of one source image within 1 minute, and adapt to new lots, equipment, or styles without retraining or with only minimal addition of normal references.
input.constraints: Input is 6559x6559 grayscale source image, Reference images are defect-free normal patches (256x256 or 512x512 or smaller), Input and output image h/w dimensions may differ, Defect annotations are very limited or absent, Training should be minimized (normal-only / few-shot / training-light preferred), Must run on single GPU or limited compute environment, Inspection time per source image must be under 1 minute, Must be robust to image style / contrast / noise level changes, Avoid end-to-end fine-tuning of very large foundation models, Must avoid excessive false positive increase for real-world deployment
input.target_metric: False Positive Rate on Normal Images, False Alarm per Image, Anomalous Area Ratio on Normal Images, Score Stability under Style/Noise Perturbation (Mean Absolute Score Shift and Normal Acceptance Rate under Perturbation), Threshold Robustness, Inference Time per Source Image, Peak Memory Usage, Throughput
